nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, commonly found over areas of mixed coral, rubble, consolidated limestone, and sand on seaward reefs, and sometimes in soft coral or sponge habitats. Depth range runs from roughly 3 to 60 m, with most activity recorded between 5 and 50 m.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (5+ cm) for nocturnal burying
well-established live rock with abundant microfauna
open swimming space along the tank's length
tightly fitted lid to prevent jumping

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on small benthic invertebrates including foraminiferans, amphipods, copepods, and small hard-shelled prey picked from rubble and sand. In captivity most newly-imported specimens refuse prepared foods initially and rely on grazing live microfauna from established live rock; live saltwater feeder shrimp, vitamin-enriched live brine and mysis, and other small live foods are commonly used to start them feeding before transitioning to frozen mysis, brine, and finely chopped marine meats. A connected refugium and a mature, microfauna-rich system are generally advised.

avoidWith: hawkfish and other boisterous tankmates that out-compete at feeding

summary: The Spotted Tamarin Wrasse is widely regarded as a specialist-only fish owing to high shipping mortality, an initial refusal of prepared foods, and dependence on a mature, microfauna-rich live-rock system with a deep fine sand bed.
careNotes: The Spotted Tamarin Wrasse, also widely sold as the Yellowtail Wrasse, is widely regarded as expert-only. Shipping is reported to be particularly stressful for Anampses because confinement in a transit bag prevents their normal stress response of burrowing, and new arrivals have been known to injure their mouths attempting to escape the bag; smaller imports of roughly 4–10 cm tend to acclimate better than larger fish. A well-established reef system (commonly cited as 12–18 months or older) with copious copepod and amphipod populations is generally considered essential, ideally supported by a connected refugium. A deep, fine sand bed of at least 5 cm is generally advised, as these fish bury themselves at night and dive into the substrate when startled. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended — Anampses are known to jump when spooked. This species is best introduced before any potentially aggressive tankmates such as tangs, hawkfish, damsels, or angelfish. Adults are dark-bodied, covered in white spots of varying sizes, with a bright yellow tail; sexual dimorphism is relatively subtle in this species compared with other Anampses, though males tend toward bolder colouration as they mature. Like other wrasses in the family Labridae, Anampses meleagrides is generally treated as a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first, meaning the dominant individual in a group can transition to male); keepers typically maintain a single specimen per tank unless working with a clearly compatible pair. Anampses are kept in non-copper-treated systems at major wholesalers, suggesting standard caution with copper-based medications even though strict copper sensitivity is not as well documented as in dragonets or sharks.
breedingNotes: Anampses meleagrides is oviparous, spawning in distinct pairs as is typical of the family Labridae. Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Yellowtail Wrasse (Expert Only)
primary.notes: Direct slug https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/spotted-tamarin-wrasse returned 404; LiveAquaria sells this species under the trade name 'Yellowtail Wrasse'. Source confirms Anampses meleagrides, 9" max size, 125 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, carnivore, peaceful, reef compatible, Expert Only, sand bed required, jumping risk.

additional.name: FishBase — Anampses meleagrides
additional.notes: Confirmed family Labridae / order Eupercaria, max length 22.0 cm TL, depth range 3–60 m (usual 5–50 m), preferred temperature 24.3–29 °C (mean 27.6 °C — averaged with LiveAquaria/Aquatics Unlimited 22.2–25.6 °C to arrive at 22–26 °C), Indo-Pacific distribution from the Red Sea and East Africa east to Samoa and the Tuamotu Islands, north to southern Japan, reef-associated habitat (mixed coral, rubble, sand, soft coral, sponge), oviparous with distinct pairing, IUCN Least Concern, trophic level 3.5.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Species Spotlight — Tamarin Wrasse of the Genus Anampses
additional.notes: Genus-wide care guidance: most Anampses considered Expert Only; high shipping stress because confinement prevents burrowing and fish often injure their mouths in the bag; small imports (~1.5–4 in) acclimate best; deep soft-substrate bed of at least 2 in (~5 cm) required; species should be the primary display fish in a large established system without aggressive tankmates; LiveAquaria houses Anampses in non-copper-treated systems; pharyngeal-jaw chew-and-spit feeding behaviour.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited — Spotted Tamarin / Yellow Tail Wrasse
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size ~8" (~20 cm), minimum 90 gal with 3" sandbed, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, carnivore, peaceful, reef safe, care level Advanced, native range Great Barrier Reef to Indo-Pacific west to Red Sea, juveniles dark with white spots and yellow tail, fine sand substrate required, difficulty getting them to accept prepared foods. Tank-size spread across sources (50/55/75/90/125 gal) — chose 450 L (~120 gal) per the cautious-larger rule given strong jumping behaviour, need for open swimming space, and active foraging lifestyle.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby literature. The species is oviparous, forming distinct pairs during spawning, and is considered a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning females can transition to male — as is typical of the family Labridae.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: High shipping mortality typical of Anampses; new arrivals frequently refuse prepared foods and depend on a mature, microfauna-rich live-rock system with a deep fine sand bed for nocturnal burying. Not appropriate for new or undersized reef systems.
